text: The dog's water bowl was still by the back door three weeks later, and no one could bring themselves to put it away, so it just stayed there gathering a thin film of dust
He found his father's reading glasses in the glove compartment of the car they were selling, lenses still smudged with thumbprints, and he put them back and closed the compartment again
The last kid on the block moved away in August and the cul-de-sac where they used to play kickball was just driveways and sprinklers now, running on timers for nobody
She scrolled past his name in her contacts and realized she had no reason to call him anymore and probably never would again but could not bring herself to delete the number
The diner where they used to eat breakfast every Sunday was now a phone repair shop and the booths and the counter and the little jukebox at each table were all just gone
At the nursing home his mother introduced him to the staff as a nice young man who was visiting, and he smiled and shook their hands and did not correct her
The acceptance letter from the program he had wanted for years arrived two days after the deadline to respond because the post office had sent it to the wrong address
She packed up the nursery slowly, folding the tiny clothes back into the drawer, peeling the wall decals off one by one, the room returning to the blank white it had been before
Rain collected in the hull of the rowboat that nobody used anymore, the one his grandfather had built, and the wood was soft now and gray and pulling away from the nails
The voicemail from his wife was still saved on his phone, just her saying she was picking up milk and would be home by six, and sometimes he played it just to hear the ordinary sound of her voice
On the last day of school the teacher stood at the door and watched the hallway empty out, the lockers standing open, someone's forgotten lunch bag on the floor by the water fountain
He drove past the house where he grew up and someone had cut down the oak tree in the front yard, the one with the tire swing, and the stump was already level with the grass
The flight home was half empty and she watched the city where she had spent four years shrink to a grid of lights and then disappear under a layer of clouds
His daughter's handprint in plaster from kindergarten was on the shelf next to the car keys she had just used to drive herself to college and would not be back for months
The restaurant brought out a cake for their anniversary and they both sang along and smiled, but the table between them felt wider than it used to
She found the list they had written together on a napkin of all the places they wanted to visit, folded inside a book on the shelf, with none of them crossed off
Every morning the old man walked to the bench by the pond and sat for exactly one hour in the spot where there used to be two chairs but now there was only one
The concert tickets were still pinned to the refrigerator with a magnet, the date already past, and neither of them mentioned it or took them down
He tried on his wedding suit for the reunion and it fit perfectly everywhere except the shoulders, which were now too wide for the boy who had worn it twenty years ago

The doctor explained the results with careful, practiced kindness, and she nodded along because the words made sense individually but together they described a life that was shorter than the one she had been planning
They found the cat curled up under the porch, eyes closed, in the exact spot where the sun hit the flagstones every afternoon, and for a moment it just looked like he was sleeping
His inbox still auto-completed her email address when he typed the first letter and every time it appeared he had to delete it character by character
The retirement party had a sheet cake and a card signed by everyone and a short speech, and then he walked to his car carrying a box of desk things and the parking lot was very quiet
She stood in the grocery store holding a jar of the hot sauce he liked and then put it back on the shelf because there was no one to buy it for anymore
The snow came early that year and covered the garden she had not had time to harvest, and from the kitchen window she could see the tops of the tomato cages barely poking through the white
At the playground the swing set creaked in the wind, chains swaying slightly, and the woman on the bench nearby remembered being small enough that her feet did not touch the ground
He opened the photo album to show his kids and realized he could not remember the name of the man standing next to him in the picture, a man he had once called his closest friend

The letter she had been composing in her head for months came out as a single sentence when she finally wrote it, and even that felt like too much, so she folded it into an envelope and did not send it
Someone had left flowers at the base of the telephone pole on Maple Street, the plastic wrapping gone cloudy from weeks of weather, the stems brown and bent
The app told him he had walked 4,200 steps and all of them had been back and forth in the same apartment, the same hallway, the same kitchen, the same window looking out at the same street
She heard the neighbor's piano through the wall playing a piece her sister used to practice, the same stumble on the same measure, and she held very still until it was over
The factory closed and the parking lot where three hundred people used to park was empty by Thursday, just yellow lines on asphalt baking in the sun with dandelions pushing through the cracks
He finished the book his wife had been reading when she died, the bookmark still on page 214, and now he knew how it ended and she never would

The birthday cards kept coming from her aunt, addressed in the same round handwriting, even though the aunt had been told the new address twice and kept sending them to the old one where nobody lived anymore
In the aquarium the fish swam in their slow circuits and the child pressed his face to the glass and said they looked like him when he walked around the house at night when he couldn't sleep
The team lost in the final round and the gymnasium went quiet and the boy sat on the bench staring at his hands like they belonged to someone who had let everyone down
She donated the clothes to the thrift store in three garbage bags and drove home with the back seat empty and the hangers still swinging in the closet when she got back
The tree they had planted when they moved in was taller than the house now, its roots lifting the sidewalk, and neither of them lived there anymore to see it
He called the number and it had been disconnected, just three tones and a recording, and he stood in the kitchen holding the phone away from his ear like it had stung him
The tide was going out and it pulled the sand castle apart slowly, tower by tower, the moat filling and collapsing, and the children who built it had already gone inside for dinner
The voicemail had been saved for two years now, replayed so many times the words were memorized, and still every time the voice said see you Thursday something behind the ribs went quiet
